taskVersion.name: epu_q2_medium
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_medium")
def epu_q2_medium(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, medium_pre, medium_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
The editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have invited 1,600 of North Texas' leaders - elected and appointed - to attend a summit in Irving tomorrow and to sign a pledge to develop by this time next year a plan for a regional transit authority.Interested in finding out more about transit? Log on to DallasNews.com/opinion from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m. tomorrow to watch streaming video live from the transit summit. Or click on "Hell Freezes Over" at the same Web address and read our earlier transit editorial, as well as the Fort Worth Star-Telegram's. Also, join us on our online blog, DMN Daily, at DallasNews.com/opinion/blog and add your voice.The ChallengeThe imperative to ctoperate is so important that the editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have decided to link arms.In an unprecedented collaboration that they hope will set an example of unity and unselfishness, they have convened this regional transit summit tomorrow in Irving. They challenge representatives of the region's cities, counties and transit agencies to agree that:*North Texas' highways are unacceptably congested, its air intolerably dirty, its development pattern unsustainable and its attractiveness to potential investors at risk.*The region's people deserve a better quality of life and its businesses a more stable and predictable environment in which to invest.*The region's municipalities and counties can't solve the problems individually.*

[...TRUNCATED...]

at kind of system.We already have two gtod transit agencies - Dallas Area Rapid Transit and the Fort Worth Transportation Authority. Another transit agency is aborning in Denton County.But not all municipalities belong to the transit agencies. In fact, more don't than do. As a consequence, fewer residents are commuting by train, bus or high-occupancy vehicle than otherwise might. That translates into denser traffic, dirtier air and an unsustainable pattern ofeconomicdevelopment.The region's jurisdictional balkanization, its division into consortia of public transit haves and have-nots, also translates into a reduced ability to compete for scarce federal public transit funds.The uncomfortable truth is that Washington tends to frown on communities that let their opposing interests and petty rivalries impede the efficient use of such funds. It tends to smile on communities that work and plan together.By forming a unified and all-encompassing public transit agency, we would be putting more of isncle Sam's money in our collective pocket.Inaction is guaranteed to make the problem persist. That's because of the region's phenomenal population growth. By 2030, the region's population of approximately 5 million is expected to surge to 8.4 million.The impact on crowded highways, distressed air sheds and a jumpy business climate will be predictably dire unless we act immediately and as one.Caption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (MICHAEL HOGisE/Staff illustration) Roads
TARGET ARTICLE END
